result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a decision becomes irreversible. Not the decision itself, but the suspended split-second when every path is still hypothetically open, like holding your breath at the top of a swing. There’s a hush in that instant that feels almost holy, the way libraries feel at closing time: knowledge still present, but already receding into the dark.

When I was nine I believed the universe kept a literal card catalog of every possible me. Each card was a life I could step into the way you slide a book from its shelf. If I jumped off the garage roof with a plastic grocery bag for a parachute, I would feel the card’s edge between my fingers: one branch labeled “broken ankle,” another “miracle flight,” another “nothing happens, just embarrassment.” I could almost hear the soft thud of the drawer sliding shut once I chose. That early, I sensed that choice was less about what you grabbed and more about what you returned to the file, the titles you never checked out disappearing quietly into dust.

Years later I learned the physicists call it the “many-worlds interpretation,” but by then the poetry had already gone out of it for me. The textbooks spoke of wave-function collapse, superposition, decoherence—words like steel girders holding up a skyscraper of abstraction. None of them mentioned the taste of summer asphalt under bare knees or the way my friend Maya’s braces flashed like semaphore when she laughed. None admitted that the most persuasive evidence for parallel realities is nostalgia, the ache of mourning for places you never actually visited.

I think we’re terrified of admitting how small the present moment is, how laughably inadequate the bandwidth of the senses. A trillion things are happening right now inside a single fingernail—cells dividing, mitochondria sighing their ancient circular songs, electrons tunneling through walls they should never breach—and yet I reduce all that grandeur to “I need to clip my nails.” The mind is a funnel, not a camera. It narrows, mercilessly, because to remain sane it has to ignore almost everything.

Sometimes I wonder if boredom is just the pressure of all those excluded possibilities bearing down on the edges of consciousness. A kid slumped at a cafeteria table isn’t experiencing nothing; she’s experiencing the friction of every door she can’t open, heard as a dull white roar. Adults laugh at teenage angst because we’ve forgotten the sheer acoustic force of those closed doors. We’ve installed soundproofing: careers, routines, the narcotic glow of habit. We call the silence “maturity,” but a part of me suspects it’s just tinnitus.

I read once that octopuses taste with their skin. I keep returning to that sentence the way a tongue keeps prodding a chipped tooth. Imagine: every surface a sentence, every touch a flavor. You could never shake hands without learning something private, could never lie in a bathtub without drinking, in some small way, your own story. Boundaries would smear; identity would leak out in clouds of chemicals. We congratulate ourselves on being upright, verbal, future-planning, but we pay for the upgrade by living inside bell jars. Perry-black waves of sensation break against the glass, and we note the pattern with our eyes, untouched.

Last week I took a night walk through the grid of streets behind the old train yard. Sodium lamps flickered like faulty metronomes, and every lawn exhaled chlorophyll coolness. A cat watched me from the shadow of a recycling bin; its eyes were green coins dropped in a dark well. Somewhere a window was open to a talk-show host reassuring nobody in particular that we were, in fact, living in unprecedented times. I thought: every era believes it invented the weather. Pompeii thought the sky falling was personal, too.

Partway down the block I passed a house where a boy practiced saxophone. The notes stumbled, caught, and soared in exactly the way breathing stutters before it becomes crying. I stopped, pretending to tie a shoe I wasn’t wearing, and let the broken solo braid itself into my pulse. Nothing improved; no revelation arrived. Still, when he finally found the melody—some standard I almost recognized—the streetlight above me seemed to bow its head in acknowledgment. For a few measures, failure and grace took turns wearing the same skin, and the distinction felt small enough to fit inside a single heartbeat.

I keep thinking about the phrase “in the middle of nowhere.” As if somewhere were a fixed address and nowhere its shabby suburb. But stand still long enough and every dot on the map becomes the center; every nowhere pivots, without warning, into the bull’s-eye of here. The cartographers know it, which is why they draw compasses like ornate flowers: north, south, east, west—petals opening endlessly around the hinge of you.

There’s a crack in my bedroom wall shaped uncannily like the state of Missouri. I’ve watched it widen by degrees each year, a shy glacier of plaster migrating south. One day it’ll reach the baseboard and divide the room into two hostile nations, each claiming the window. I picture myself applying for visas to cross from bed to dresser, brandishing a passport stamped with faded laundry tickets. I should patch it; I never do. Maybe I’m waiting to see how far a fault line can travel when nobody intervenes, whether continents feel this same lazy entitlement as they drift, crashing into each other like drunks on a dance floor, forming mountains no one asked for.

I keep thinking about the word “archive.” How it contains both ardor and ache. How every attempt to remember is also an act of cremation, preserving the shape while the heat destroys the scent. I own a shoebox of ticket stubs: concerts, ferries, museum exhibits visited on rainy afternoons when I pretended to be someone more curious than afraid. The ink has gone faint, like a voice giving up on the far end of a phone line. I could scan them, upload to a cloud, ensure persistence; instead I let them yellow, taking comfort in the slow betrayal of pigments. Fading is at least honest; it admits we were never meant to last.

Maybe that’s what the moment before decision really is: archive smoke still uncoiled, ash still waiting to decide what it once was. Maybe jumping from the garage roof was never about flight or injury, but about holding the card so long it warms in your hand, evidence that for one bright second you were the entire library, every story still possible, not yet shelved in the dim rows of what turned out to be.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
